title: 狗公园规划设计
background:
2021 MidMCM
Problem C: Dog Park
Note: Only teams with all members younger than 14½ years old may choose Problem C

Dog parks are becoming increasingly popular in the United States and Canada. The city of
Calgary in Alberta, Canada has 10 dog parks for a city of 1.3 million residents, and the city of
Boise in Idaho, US has 6.3 dog parks per 100,000 residents!

Considering the benefits they provide to dogs, their owners, and communities, the growth in
demand for dog parks is not surprising. A dog park is typically a fenced-in outdoor area where
people and their dogs can play together. Dog parks allow dogs to roam, exercise, and play off-
leash in a safe and controlled environment. It’s like a playground for dogs. Moreover, dog
owners are free to socialize while their dogs are playing. This can help new residents establish
community connections and allows existing neighbors to stay connected.

Much like playgrounds, dog parks come in many shapes and sizes depending on the space and
money available. With enough space comes the option to have separate spaces for large and
small dogs. Some owners prefer this separation, while others prefer to have dogs of all sizes play
together. Some dog parks have lighting, while others only operate during daylight hours. Basic
dog parks may have little more than a fenced in open space, while other more elaborate dog
parks may have a wide variety of amenities and dog play equipment.

The Town of Middleville Director of Parks & Recreation has asked your team to develop a plan
to build the Middleville Dog Park at the site shown in Figure 1. The Director provided the
following guidance:

1. You may locate the dog park anywhere within the area outlined in blue in Figure 1.
2. The dog park may be up to 1-acre (43,560 square feet) in total size.
3. You must keep the existing skateboard park in its current location.

| ©2021 by COMAP, Inc. | www.comap.com | www.mathmodels.org | @comapmath | info@comap.com |

 Figure 1: Area for Location of Dog Park

1. Planning Your Dog Park

a. The necessary features of a safe dog park are fencing, a water supply, and trash cans.
There are many other features that you might want to include in your dog park. Make a list of
all the features you want to consider for your dog park.

b. Now think about the decisions you must make to plan and design your dog park. A few of
your questions might be:

•  What shape and how big will the dog park be?
•  Where in the available area (Figure 1) will you locate your dog park?
•  What materials will you need to build the park?
•  How much will it cost to build and maintain the dog park?

Make a list of questions you need to ask or decisions you need to make prior to designing
your dog park.

c. Describe your dog park. To do this you will need to decide what features you will include,
answer your questions, and make necessary decisions (from your work in Parts 1.a. and 1.b.).

| ©2021 by COMAP, Inc. | www.comap.com | www.mathmodels.org | @comapmath | info@comap.com |

 2. Designing Your Dog Park

a. Make a scale drawing of your dog park that includes all the features you decide to include.
Label your drawing with measurements and be sure to note your scale. You can hand-draw
your dog park or use technology to do so.

b. Builders often use a Bill-Of-Materials (BOM) to list the materials and items needed for a
project, along with quantities and costs. An example of a BOM with a sample entry is shown
in Table 1.

Bill-Of-Materials

Item
Number

1.

Description and Size

Fence Gate, Chain-Link,
5-ft tall × 4.5-ft wide

Amount
Needed

Cost per
Item

Total Cost

2

$100

$200

Table 1: Sample Entry for Bill-Of-Materials (BOM)

Use your scale drawing to create a BOM for your dog park project.

c. In addition to the cost of the materials and items shown in your BOM, list and describe any
other expenses necessary to build and maintain your dog park.

d. Sometimes after developing your plan, you need to make some adjustments. What if
Middleville decided to provide more or less space for the dog park than you planned? What if
they provided more or less money than you decided you needed to build the park? Rather
than starting over, how could you adjust your existing dog park plan? Write a paragraph
about how you could generalize your dog park plan so that it is scalable.

3. Sharing your dog park plan

Write a one-page letter to the Middleville Director of Parks & Recreation describing your dog
park and its features. Address the scalability of your dog park.

Your MidMCM PDF solution document should include the following:

•  One-page Summary Sheet.
•  Table of Contents.
•  Your complete solution to the problem and requirements.
•  One-page letter to the Middleville Director of Parks & Recreation.
•  References List (for example, any websites you used to gather information).

There is no specific required page length for a complete MidMCM submission. You may use up
to 25 total pages for all your solution work and any additional information you want to include
(for example: drawings, diagrams, calculations, tables). Partial solutions are accepted.

| ©2021 by COMAP, Inc. | www.comap.com | www.mathmodels.org | @comapmath | info@comap.com |

 Glossary

Assumption: a hypothesis or educated guess that takes the place of an unknown piece of
information.

Bill-Of-Materials (BOM): a complete list of all required parts, items, raw materials, etc.
needed to complete a given project, along with their costs.

Generalize: make something less specific.

Maintain: keep clean and safe over time.

Scalable: the ability to change something in size or scale (for example, make larger or
smaller).

Scale Drawing: a drawing of a real object that has been made smaller or larger
proportionally. The proportion used in the drawing is called the scale ratio.

Guidance for our First Annual MidMCM.

As this is the first year of MidMCM, COMAP does not have example papers online. Therefore,
we provide the following general guidance about submission organization.

Solutions must be in PDF format and submitted in one PDF document. However, this does not
preclude MidMCM teams from doing mathematics, graphs, tables, sketches, etc. by hand and
including pictures of their work in the single PDF document submission. As students move to
high school and the HiMCM, we expect that submissions will be typed. For the MidMCM,
advisors may assist students in putting their solution components into one PDF format file for
submission.

As with HiMCM, there is a 25-page limit for the submission document. This does not mean your
solution must be 25 pages. All portions of your submission (text, graphs, tables, charts, pictures,
etc.) must be within one PDF document that is 25 pages or less. Partial solutions are accepted.

In general, a complete solution submission is organized as follows:

Executive Summary – Write this summary after you have done all of your work. This one-page
summary is Page #1 of your solution document. It provides an overview of your work and
includes actual results.

Table of Contents – List the major items in your solution document to show the organization of
your paper.

Introduction and Restatement of the Problem – Introduce the problem. Restate the problem
and requirements in your own words.

| ©2021 by COMAP, Inc. | www.comap.com | www.mathmodels.org | @comapmath | info@comap.com |

 Assumptions with Justifications – State any assumptions you made to simplify and solve the
problem and state why you made those assumptions. For example:
Assumption: Our dog park fence will be 5 feet tall.
Justification: After researching dog parks online, we found most dog parks had fences

between 4 feet and 6 feet tall. We chose to use 5 feet tall.

Variable Definitions – Define any variables you use in your work, drawing, and solution.
For example: A = the enclosed area of the dog park measured in square feet.

Presentation of Model and Solution – Ensure you address all requirements and describe what
you are doing to solve the problem. Show and explain all your work. Use representations that
help you tell the reader how you solved the problem (for example: equations, tables, graphs,
pictures, etc.).

Analysis of Your Work – Address any strengths (good points) and limitations (weaknesses) of
your solution.

Concluding Paragraph – End your solution paper with a final concluding paragraph that
summarizes your results and/or makes recommendations for future work.

Reference List – List any sources that you used to solve the problem (for example, website
pages, newspaper or magazine articles, etc.).

| ©2021 by COMAP, Inc. | www.comap.com | www.mathmodels.org | @comapmath | info@comap.com |

steps.guide_style: 苏格拉底式追问，引导分点列举，不直接给答案
steps.deliverable: 子问题清单 + 关键约束
steps.title: 问题分析与重述
steps.prompt: 阅读题目，用自己的话重述问题：你需要为Middleville镇设计一个狗公园，场地限制在蓝色区域内，最大面积1英亩（43560平方英尺），必须保留现有滑板公园。列出所有需要完成的任务（如规划特征、设计图纸、材料清单、成本、可扩展性等）。

criteria.dim: 正确理解并重述了问题的主要目标
列出了所有关键任务（规划、设计、成本、可扩展性、信件）
语言清晰，无遗漏

steps.reference_outline: 我们需要为Middleville镇设计一个狗公园，场地在蓝色区域内，最大面积1英亩，滑板公园位置不变。任务包括：列出所需特征（如围栏、水源、垃圾桶等）；提出设计问题（形状、位置、材料、成本）；描述公园；制作比例图纸；创建材料清单（BOM）；列出其他费用；讨论可扩展性；最后给主管写一封信。
steps.hint: 注意题目中分成了三个部分：Planning、Designing、Sharing，每个部分都有具体子问题。

steps.guide_style: 质疑每条假设的必要性与合理性
steps.title: 模型假设
steps.prompt: 列出至少5个你在设计狗公园时需要做出的假设，并给出每个假设的合理理由。例如：围栏高度、地面材料、使用人数等。

criteria.dim: 假设数量足够（至少5个）
每个假设都有合理的理由
假设与设计相关且合理

steps.reference_outline: 1. 围栏高度5英尺：大多数狗公园围栏4-6英尺，5英尺安全且经济。2. 地面为草地：常见且成本低。3. 公园开放时间6:00-22:00：参考其他公园。4. 平均每只狗占地200平方英尺：保证活动空间。5. 材料价格基于当地市场：使用典型价格。
steps.hint: 考虑安全、成本、使用情况等方面，每个假设要能简化问题但不过分偏离现实。

steps.guide_style: 检查符号一致性与单位是否齐全
steps.title: 符号说明与变量定义
steps.prompt: 定义你在后续计算中会用到的变量和符号。例如：$A$ = 公园面积（平方英尺），$L$ = 围栏长度（英尺），$C$ = 总成本（美元）。至少定义5个变量，并说明单位。

criteria.dim: 变量定义清晰，包含单位
变量数量足够（至少5个）
变量与后续模型一致

steps.reference_outline: $A$ = 公园面积（平方英尺），$P$ = 围栏周长（英尺），$N_b$ = 长椅数量，$N_t$ = 垃圾桶数量，$C_f$ = 围栏总成本（美元），$C_m$ = 维护成本（美元/年），$S$ = 可扩展比例因子。
steps.hint: 变量要覆盖面积、成本、数量等，方便后面建立方程。

steps.guide_style: 推敲方程是否自洽、可解、贴合题意
steps.title: 模型建立
steps.prompt: 根据你的假设和变量，建立数学模型来决定公园的形状、尺寸和位置。考虑最大化面积或最小化围栏长度，同时满足最大面积1英亩。写出面积和周长的公式，并解释你选择的形状（如矩形、圆形或组合形）。

criteria.dim: 选择了合理的形状并给出公式
面积计算正确，不超过1英亩
考虑了位置约束（靠近停车场、远离滑板公园）
模型有清晰的数学表达

steps.reference_outline: 我们选择矩形加半圆组合形状：中间矩形长$l$，宽$w$，两端半圆半径$r=w/2$。总面积$A = lw + \pi r^2$，周长$P = 2l + 2\pi r$。令$A=43560$平方英尺，取$r=78$英尺，则$l = (43560 - \pi \times 78^2)/156 \approx 120$英尺。位置选在蓝色区域靠近停车场一侧，远离滑板公园。
steps.hint: 考虑用微积分优化形状，但初中生可以用试错法。注意围栏长度影响成本。

steps.guide_style: 逐步调试，关注可运行性与结果合理
steps.title: 模型求解
steps.prompt: 根据你建立的模型，计算具体尺寸、围栏长度、所需材料数量（如围栏板、门、长椅、垃圾桶等）。然后制作一个材料清单（BOM），包括项目、描述、数量、单价和总价。假设围栏每英尺$15，门每个$100，长椅每个$200，垃圾桶每个$50。

criteria.dim: 尺寸计算正确
BOM表格完整，包含所有主要材料
成本计算正确
单位一致，计算过程清晰

steps.reference_outline: 由步骤4，$r=78$英尺，$l=120$英尺，周长$P=2\times120+2\pi\times78\approx730$英尺。围栏成本：$730\times15=10950$美元。门2个：$200$美元。长椅4个：$800$美元。垃圾桶4个：$200$美元。总材料成本$12150$美元。BOM表格略。
steps.hint: 注意围栏长度要包括门的位置，门可以算入围栏长度的一部分。

steps.guide_style: 追问结果是否可信、与现实是否吻合
steps.title: 结果分析与检验
steps.prompt: 检查你的设计是否满足所有约束：面积≤1英亩，位置在蓝色区域内，滑板公园保留。同时评估你的设计是否合理（如是否足够容纳预期数量的狗和主人）。假设每只狗需要200平方英尺，计算最大容量。

criteria.dim: 验证了面积约束
验证了位置和滑板公园约束
计算了容量并讨论合理性
结果分析有逻辑

steps.reference_outline: 面积$A=43560$平方英尺，等于1英亩，满足。位置在蓝色区域内，远离滑板公园。最大容量：$43560/200\approx218$只狗，但实际考虑主人和设施，建议容量100只狗。设计合理。
steps.hint: 容量计算是粗略估计，实际还要考虑通道和设施占用面积。

steps.guide_style: 引导设计扰动实验并解读
steps.title: 灵敏度分析
steps.prompt: 考虑如果预算减少20%或面积减少20%，你的设计如何调整？讨论你的设计是否可扩展（scalable）。例如，如果面积减半，你如何修改形状和材料？

criteria.dim: 分析了预算变化的影响
分析了面积变化的影响
提出了具体的调整方案
讨论了可扩展性

steps.reference_outline: 如果预算减少20%，可减少长椅数量或使用更便宜的材料。如果面积减少20%，可缩小半径和长度，保持形状比例。例如，面积减半至21780平方英尺，可令$r=55$英尺，$l=85$英尺。设计具有可扩展性，因为形状简单，可按比例缩放。
steps.hint: 可扩展性意味着你的设计可以轻松调整大小，而不需要完全重新设计。

steps.guide_style: 对照假设找局限，避免空泛
steps.title: 模型评价
steps.prompt: 列出你的模型的优点和缺点。优点如简单、成本低；缺点如未考虑地形、天气等。至少各写2点。

criteria.dim: 列出了至少2个优点
列出了至少2个缺点
评价客观合理

steps.reference_outline: 优点：1. 形状简单，易于建造和扩展。2. 成本估算清晰，便于预算。缺点：1. 未考虑地面排水和坡度。2. 未考虑不同天气下的使用情况。
steps.hint: 从实际应用角度思考，比如安全性、舒适性、维护等。
